d2.issue: Whether a patent grant from the State of Georgia for 7,300 acres of land was void and inadmissible as evidence because Georgia law allegedly prohibited grants exceeding 1,000 acres to any one person.
d2.rule: A patent may be impeached collaterally in a court of law in an action of ejectment only if it is absolutely void upon its face, was issued without authority, was prohibited by statute, or the state had no title to the land. The validity of a patent cannot be challenged for irregularities in preliminary proceedings when those proceedings were completed under proper safeguards and the patent was issued by state authority.
d2.application: The Court examined Georgia's land laws and determined that the 1,000-acre limitation in the Act of February 17, 1783 applied only to head rights (warrants), not to final grants. The proviso limiting grants to 1,000 acres restricted only the quantity obtainable through head rights in one's own family, but did not prohibit consolidating multiple transferable warrants into a single grant exceeding 1,000 acres. The Court found evidence that warrants were transferable under Georgia law and that subsequent statutes authorized fees for grants exceeding 1,000 acres, demonstrating that such grants were lawful in 1787 when this grant issued.
d2.conclusion: The grant for 7,300 acres was not void and should have been admitted as evidence because Georgia law in 1787 did not prohibit issuing patents for more than 1,000 acres when based on consolidated warrants. The Court certified that the evidence was competent to sustain the plaintiff's case.

j3.issue: Whether a patent grant from the State of Georgia for 7,300 acres of land was void and inadmissible as evidence because Georgia law allegedly prohibited grants exceeding 1,000 acres to any one person.
j3.rule: Under the doctrine established in Polk's Lessee v. Wendell, 9 Cranch 87, and reaffirmed in 18 U.S. 5 Wheat. 293, a patent may be impeached collaterally in a court of law in an action of ejectment only if it is absolutely void upon its face, was issued without authority, was prohibited by statute, or the state had no title to the land. A grant raises a presumption that every prerequisite to its issuing was complied with, and it would be unreasonable to avoid a grant for irregularities in preliminary proceedings when those proceedings were completed under proper safeguards and the patent was issued by state authority.
j3.application: The Court examined Georgia's land laws, particularly the Act of February 17, 1783, and determined that the 1,000-acre limitation applied only to head rights (warrants), not to final grants. The proviso limiting grants to 1,000 acres restricted only the quantity obtainable through head rights in one's own family, but did not prohibit consolidating multiple transferable warrants into a single grant exceeding 1,000 acres. The Court found evidence that warrants were transferable under Georgia law prior to 1794, and that subsequent statutes authorized fees for grants exceeding 1,000 acres (Acts of December 23, 1789 and December 18, 1792), demonstrating that such grants were lawful in 1787 when this grant issued. The grant was based on consolidated old warrants, a practice consistent with Georgia's land law system.
j3.conclusion: The grant for 7,300 acres was not void and should have been admitted as evidence because Georgia law in 1787 did not prohibit issuing patents for more than 1,000 acres when based on consolidated warrants. The Court certified that the evidence was competent to sustain the plaintiff's case, establishing that statutory interpretation must consider the entire statutory scheme and that limitations on warrants do not necessarily apply to final grants.
citations: Act of July 17, 1783, Prince's Dig. 266. sec. 36
